medications.indication_rationale: Relieves colicky smooth-muscle spasm in a functional/coeliac flare; the patient already uses it. Appropriate provided surgical red flags are absent.
medications.dose_route_frequency: per standard OTC/label dosing as required (adult)

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids mask evolving surgical pathology and worsen ileus; inappropriate for undifferentiated acute abdominal pain via telehealth.
Contraindicated if obstruction is possible; not appropriate while a mechanical cause is not excluded.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once settled, adopt a strict gluten-free diet and confirm coeliac status with the GP; tonight take clear fluids and avoid further large/gluten meals.
Apply gentle heat, mobilise slowly, and monitor for the red-flag features below.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Eases gas/spasm while enabling rapid escalation if a surgical cause emerges.

patient_education_points.point: This is very likely a flare from the big gluten meal — gas and cramping from an irritated gut, which can feel alarming and bloated. Because you're still passing wind, aren't vomiting, and the pain comes in waves rather than being constant, a blockage is unlikely.
Use your antispasmodic, sip clear fluids, apply gentle heat and move around a little; it usually eases over the next 12-24 hours.

Go to ED if the pain becomes constant and severe, you start vomiting (especially green/faeculent), you completely stop passing wind and motions, you develop a fever, or the pain localises to your lower right side.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP within 3-5 days (sooner if not settling)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Symptom resolution, coeliac confirmation and dietitian referral; in-person abdominal examination if pain persists.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Constant/severe or localised pain, bilious vomiting, complete obstipation, or fever — attend ED.

must_include_items: Screen obstruction/appendicitis/ischaemia red flags before attributing to a flare
Offer antispasmodic + simple measures for a functional/coeliac flare
Provide a strict surgical-abdomen safety-net and keep a low threshold for in-person review (abdomen not palpable remotely)
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day in-person assessment if the patient prefers or symptoms are worsening
Antispasmodic + clear fluids + heat as symptomatic care
errors_of_commission: Reflex midnight ED/CT for a resolving functional flare with no red flags
Prescribing opioids or a laxative for an undifferentiated acute abdomen
Reassuring without any red-flag screen or in-person threshold
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T2 case: passing requires a surgical red-flag screen PLUS symptomatic management with a clear safety-net. Both reflex CT/ED and unscreened reassurance fail.
